昭和年から西暦へ即座に変換 | Convert Showa era dates instantly

Convert any Showa (昭和) era year to the Western Gregorian calendar. The Showa era spans from 1926 to 1989, covering 64 years of Japanese history under Emperor Hirohito. Enter a Showa year below for an instant conversion.

Showa to Western Year — Quick Reference Table

Showa Year (昭和)Western YearNotes
昭和1年 (元年)1926Era began Dec 25
昭和5年1930
昭和10年1935
昭和15年1940
昭和20年1945End of WWII
昭和25年1950
昭和30年1955
昭和35年1960
昭和40年1965
昭和45年1970Osaka Expo
昭和50年1975
昭和55年1980
昭和60年1985
昭和63年1988
昭和64年1989Only Jan 1–7; then Heisei

About the Showa Era (昭和時代)

The Showa era (昭和) is the longest era in Japanese history, spanning 64 years from December 25, 1926 to January 7, 1989. Named after Emperor Hirohito, the Showa period witnessed some of the most transformative events in modern Japanese history. The era began during Japan's period of increasing militarism, leading to World War II and the devastating atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in 1945. Following Japan's surrender, the post-war years ushered in a remarkable era of rebuilding and democratization under the new constitution of 1947.

The latter half of the Showa era is defined by Japan's extraordinary economic miracle. From the rubble of wartime destruction, Japan rebuilt itself into the world's second-largest economy by the 1980s, pioneering advancements in automotive manufacturing, electronics, and technology. To convert any Showa year to the Western calendar, use the simple formula: Showa year + 1925 = Western year. For instance, Showa 40 equals 1965, and Showa 60 equals 1985. The Showa era ended on January 7, 1989, when Emperor Hirohito passed away, and the Heisei era began the following day.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I convert Showa to Western year?

To convert a Showa year to the Western (Gregorian) calendar, simply add 1925 to the Showa year number. For example, Showa 50 equals 1925 + 50 = 1975. This formula works for every year of the Showa era, from Showa 1 (1926) through Showa 64 (1989). Use the converter tool above for instant results with optional month and day precision.

When did the Showa era start and end?

The Showa era began on December 25, 1926, when Emperor Hirohito ascended the Chrysanthemum Throne following the death of Emperor Taisho. It ended on January 7, 1989, upon the passing of Emperor Hirohito. Spanning 64 years, Showa is the longest era in Japanese history. The era saw Japan through World War II, post-war reconstruction, and the economic miracle that transformed the nation into a global powerhouse.

What is Showa 64 in Western calendar?

Showa 64 corresponds to the year 1989 in the Western calendar. However, Showa 64 lasted only seven days, from January 1 to January 7, 1989. Emperor Hirohito passed away on January 7, and the Heisei era began on January 8, 1989. This means 1989 is split between two eras: Showa 64 (January 1–7) and Heisei 1 (January 8–December 31).
